title: The Way
hook: 道 is the oldest metaphor in Chinese thought — a road you walk and a pattern the world follows. A day to sit with it, from Laozi's paradoxes to the Daoist temples that keep the image alive.

title: The Heart-Mind
hook: 心 is both organ and consciousness — the seat of feeling and the seat of thought, inseparable in Chinese. Read it as a character, then read what Mencius and the Neo-Confucians made of it.

title: Spring Festival
hook: 春节 is the hinge of the Chinese year. A day on how it is kept — dumplings folded by hand, red envelopes tucked under doors, the zodiac turning over.

title: Yin & Yang
hook: 阴阳 is not a duality of good and evil — it's a grammar of complementary pairs that runs through medicine, cosmology, and the calendar. Follow it into the five phases and the solar terms.

title: Tang Poetry
hook: 唐诗 is the high-water mark of the Chinese poetic tradition. A day on the Tang dynasty that produced it, the Song lyrics that answered it, and the calligraphy that still copies its lines.

title: Face & Relation
hook: 面子 and 关系 are the two concepts most often mistranslated out of Chinese. A day on the social grammar they encode — and the everyday manners that keep both intact.

title: Confucius
hook: 孔子 shaped two thousand years of Chinese moral thought with a small book of conversations. A day on what he actually taught, and the students and critics who came after.

title: Tea
hook: 茶 is how China learned to treat drinking as an art. A day on the leaf, the ceremony, the regional cuisines it threads through, and the herbal tradition that first noticed it.

title: Chan Buddhism
hook: 禅 is what crossed the sea to become Zen. A day on the meditative school at home — its Pure Land cousin, its Buddhist roots, and the character 佛 that names them all.

title: The First Emperor
hook: 秦始皇 unified the writing system, the roads, and the weights — and built a tomb guarded by an army of clay. A day on the dynasty he founded and the Legalist thinkers who armed him.

title: Chengyu — Contradiction
hook: 矛盾 literally means 'spear and shield' — a merchant who claimed both invincible weapon and impenetrable defence. A day inside four-character idioms and the Warring States parables that shaped them.

title: Dumplings & the Table
hook: 饺子 are pleated for family and eaten at midnight on New Year's Eve. A day around the Chinese table — noodles, tofu, and the shared hotpot that still defines hospitality.
